metadata.answer:
## Cassandra vs HBase vs BigTable Trade-offs

### Architecture Comparison

| | Cassandra | HBase | BigTable |
|---|---|---|---|
| Replication | Distributed ring | Rack-aware | Google proprietary |
| Consistency | Eventual tunable | Strong | Strong |
| Failure | Graceful (no SPOF) | Master election | Google infra |
| Latency | 10-50ms | 1-5ms | 1-5ms |
| Write | 50K+ QPS | 100K+ QPS | 1M+ QPS |

### When to Use

**Cassandra**:
- Distributed, no single point of failure
- Geo-distributed reads needed
- High write throughput
- Examples: Netflix, Spotify

**HBase**:
- Strong consistency required
- Hadoop ecosystem needed
- Write-heavy, few reads
- Examples: Facebook, Yahoo

**BigTable**:
- Google infrastructure only
- Extreme scale (petabytes+)
- Managed service (zero ops)
- Examples: Google Analytics, Maps

### Cost (1 year, 10 nodes)
- Cassandra: $500K hardware + ops
- HBase: $500K + Hadoop + ops
- BigTable: $500K Google service (no ops)

### Query Capabilities
- Cassandra: CQL (SQL-like), limited joins
- HBase: Scan/Get, external indexes
- BigTable: Scan/Get, Dataflow integration

### Migration Path
```
PostgreSQL → Cassandra/HBase (millions QPS)
          → BigTable (petabyte scale)
```

q6.label: Column Family Design & Optimization
q6.description: How do you design column families for performance at scale?

metadata.tags: design/pattern, interview-technical, pattern/design, performance/caching, performance/compression, performance/optimization
metadata.mermaid:
graph TB CF1["Column Family 1
profile"] CF2["Column Family 2
settings"] Compress["Compression
LZ4"] BloomFilter["Bloom Filter
Fast Lookup"] Cache["Row Key Cache
Memory"] CF1 -->|Optimize| Compress CF2 -->|Optimize| BloomFilter CF1 -->|Speed| Cache Compress -->|50-70% reduction| CF1 BloomFilter -->|Avoid disk I/O| CF1 style CF1 fill:#c8e6c9 style CF2 fill:#a5d6a7 style Compress fill:#81c784 style BloomFilter fill:#66bb6a style Cache fill:#4caf50
metadata.answer:
## Column Family Design & Optimization

### Column Family Grouping

**Functional Grouping**:
```
CF: profile (name, email, bio)
CF: settings (theme, language, notifications)
```
Optimize: Settings change rarely

**Access Pattern Grouping**:
```
CF: aggregate (count, sum, avg)
CF: details (full json)
```
Optimize: Reports read aggregates only

**TTL Grouping**:
```
CF: live (TTL=7 days)
CF: historical (TTL=1 year)
```
Optimize: Different compaction policies

### Optimization Techniques

**Compression**:
```cql
ALTER TABLE metrics WITH
  compression = { 'sstable_compression' : 'LZ4Compressor' };
```
Savings: 50-70% for numeric data

**Bloom Filters**:
```cql
ALTER TABLE WITH bloom_filter_fp_chance = 0.01;  -- 1% false positive
```
Trade: More memory, fewer disk accesses

**Caching**:
```cql
ALTER TABLE WITH caching = { 'keys': 'ALL' };
```

**Compaction Strategy**:
```cql
ALTER TABLE WITH compaction = {
  'class': 'TimeWindowCompactionStrategy',
  'compaction_window_unit': 'DAYS'
};
```

### Anti-patterns
- ❌ Unbounded partition (millions columns)
- ❌ Mix hot/cold in same CF
- ❌ High cardinality partition key
- ✅ Denormalize for access pattern
- ✅ Query-first schema design

### Partition Sizing
- Small: 50 MB - 1 GB
- Medium: 1-10 GB
- Large: 10-50 GB
- Optimal: < 100 MB per partition

### Performance Checklist
- [ ] Row key avoids hotspots
- [ ] Partition size < 100 MB
- [ ] 2-3 CFs per table
- [ ] Compression enabled for text
- [ ] Bloom filters tuned
- [ ] TTL for time-series
- [ ] Correct compaction strategy

q3.label: Sharding MongoDB for Horizontal Scaling
q3.description: How do you shard MongoDB collections across multiple servers?

metadata.tags: algorithm/hash, database/mongodb, interview-technical, interview/architecture, language/javascript, quality/management, scaling/sharding, system/split
metadata.mermaid:
graph TB Client["Client"] Mongos["Mongos
Router"] Shard1["Shard 1
user_id: 1-100M"] Shard2["Shard 2
user_id: 100M-200M"] Shard3["Shard 3
user_id: 200M-300M"] Client -->|Query| Mongos Mongos -->|Route| Shard1 Mongos -->|Route| Shard2 Mongos -->|Route| Shard3 style Client fill:#f3e5f5 style Mongos fill:#e1bee7 style Shard1 fill:#ce93d8 style Shard2 fill:#ba68c8 style Shard3 fill:#ab47bc
metadata.answer:
## Sharding MongoDB for Horizontal Scaling

### Architecture
```
Client → Mongos (router pool) → Shard 1 (Replica Set)
                            ├→ Shard 2 (Replica Set)
                            └→ Shard 3 (Replica Set)

Config Servers (metadata)
```

### Shard Key Selection

**Good**:
- High cardinality (millions unique)
- Distributed writes
- Frequently queried

**Bad**:
```javascript
// LOW: 200 countries → uneven
db.users.shardCollection("users", { country: 1 });

// HOTSPOT: All new data sequential
db.events.shardCollection("events", { timestamp: 1 });

// GOOD: Unique, distributed
db.users.shardCollection("users", { user_id: 1 });
```

### Sharding Strategies

**Range-Based**:
```
Shard 1: 1-100M
Shard 2: 100M-200M
```
Pros: Range queries work
Cons: Can create hotspots

**Hash-Based**:
```
Shard = hash(user_id) % num_shards
Even distribution guaranteed
```
Pros: No hotspots
Cons: Range queries slow

**Hash Sharding** (Recommended):
```javascript
db.users.shardCollection("users", { user_id: "hashed" });
```

### Chunk Management
- 64 MB default chunk size
- Auto-split when > 64 MB
- Auto-migrate when unbalanced

### Balancer
```javascript
db.adminCommand("balancerStatus");
db.collection.getShardDistribution();
db.adminCommand({ balancerStop: 1 });
```

### Anti-patterns
- ❌ Shard on timestamp
- ❌ Monotonic shard key
- ❌ Change shard key later
- ✅ Hash sharding for most
- ✅ Plan before hitting limit
- ✅ Monitor chunk distribution

q4.label: Replication Sets & High Availability
q4.description: How do MongoDB replica sets ensure data durability and availability?

metadata.tags: concept/async, data/replication, database/mongodb, indexing/latency, interview-technical, interview/architecture, system/availability, system/failover, system/monitoring
metadata.mermaid:
graph TB Primary["Primary
Writes"] Secondary1["Secondary 1
Read-only"] Secondary2["Secondary 2
Read-only"] Arbiter["Arbiter
Voting"] Election["Election
Quorum"] Primary -->|Replicate| Secondary1 Primary -->|Replicate| Secondary2 Secondary1 -->|Heartbeat| Arbiter Secondary2 -->|Heartbeat| Arbiter Secondary1 -->|Vote| Election Secondary2 -->|Vote| Election Arbiter -->|Vote| Election style Primary fill:#fff3e0 style Secondary1 fill:#ffe0b2 style Secondary2 fill:#ffd54f style Arbiter fill:#ffca28 style Election fill:#ffc107
metadata.answer:
## Replication Sets & High Availability

### Architecture
```
Primary (writes, reads optional)
  ├── Secondary (async replication, read-only)
  ├── Secondary
  └── Arbiter (voting only, no data)

Typical lag: < 1 second
```

### Read Preference

```javascript
// PRIMARY (default)
db.collection.find().readPreference("primary");

// SECONDARY_PREFERRED (read from secondary, fallback primary)
db.collection.find().readPreference("secondaryPreferred");

// NEAREST (lowest latency node)
db.collection.find().readPreference("nearest");
```

### Write Concern
```javascript
// Majority acknowledged
db.collection.insertOne(doc, { writeConcern: { w: "majority" } });

// Journaled to disk
db.collection.insertOne(doc, { writeConcern: { j: true } });

// Fire-and-forget (dangerous)
db.collection.insertOne(doc, { writeConcern: { w: 0 } });
```

### Replica Set States
- **Primary**: Handles reads/writes
- **Secondary**: Replicates primary
- **Arbiter**: Voting only
- **Hidden**: Secondary not visible to clients
- **Delayed**: Secondary with lag for data protection

### Failover
```
1. Primary unavailable
2. Secondaries detect (heartbeat)
3. Election triggered
4. Majority vote new primary
5. Clients auto-redirect

Failover time: 10-30 seconds
```

### Configuration
```javascript
rs.initiate({
  _id: "rs0",
  members: [
    { _id: 0, host: "mongo1:27017", priority: 1 },  // Primary
    { _id: 1, host: "mongo2:27017", priority: 0 },  // Secondary
    { _id: 2, host: "mongo3:27017", priority: 0 }   // Secondary
  ]
});
```

### Monitoring
```javascript
rs.status();  // Full status
rs.printSecondaryReplicationInfo();  // Replication lag
```

### Best Practices
- [ ] 3-node minimum (1 primary + 2 secondary)
- [ ] Monitor lag < 1 second
- [ ] Use majority write concern for critical ops
- [ ] Never use even-numbered sets
- [ ] Test failover regularly

q6.label: MongoDB vs Relational Databases Trade-offs
q6.description: When is MongoDB better than SQL databases?

metadata.tags: data/sharding, database/mongodb, database/postgresql, design/decision, interview-technical, language/javascript, performance/throughput, system/consistency
metadata.mermaid:
graph LR
    A["Option A"]
    B["Comparison"]

    D["Trade-offs"]
    E["Decision"]
    A --> B --> D --> E
    C --> B
    style A fill:#bbdefb
    style C fill:#bbdefb
    style E fill:#1976d2
metadata.answer:
## MongoDB vs Relational Databases Trade-offs

### Feature Matrix

| | MongoDB | PostgreSQL |
|---|---|---|
| Model | Document (flexible) | Relational (structured) |
| Schema | Schemaless | Predefined |
| Joins | $lookup (slower) | Native (fast) |
| ACID | Multi-doc (4.0+) | Full ACID |
| Query | MongoDB QL | SQL |
| Scaling | Horizontal (sharding) | Vertical + replicas |
| Normalization | Denormalize | Normalize 3NF |

### When to Use MongoDB

**Use if**:
- Schema evolves frequently
- Flexible nested data
- Horizontal scaling needed
- High write throughput
- Document-centric

Examples: CMS, user profiles, real-time analytics

**Don't use if**:
- Complex multi-table joins
- Strict consistency required
- Normalization important
- SQL analytics needed

### When to Use PostgreSQL

**Use if**:
- Relational data, many joins
- ACID critical (financial)
- Complex queries needed
- Normalization reduces duplication
- Full SQL capability

Examples: Banking, ecommerce, analytics

### Data Model Example

**MongoDB** (denormalized):
```javascript
db.users.insertOne({
  name: "John",
  orders: [
    { id: 101, items: [{sku: "A", qty: 2}] },
    { id: 102, items: [{sku: "B", qty: 1}] }
  ]
});
```

**PostgreSQL** (normalized):
```sql
CREATE TABLE users (id, name);
CREATE TABLE orders (id, user_id, total);
CREATE TABLE order_items (id, order_id, sku);

SELECT u.name, o.id, oi.sku
FROM users u
JOIN orders o ON u.id = o.user_id
JOIN order_items oi ON o.id = oi.order_id;
```

### Cost (1 TB)
- MongoDB: $10K hardware + ops OR $500+/month cloud
- PostgreSQL: $5K hardware + ops OR $200+/month cloud

### Decision
- **Flexible schema + scale**: MongoDB
- **Complex queries**: PostgreSQL
- **Team preference**: Choose that
- **Analytics**: PostgreSQL
- **Real-time**: MongoDB (better horizontal)

q1.label: MySQL Architecture & Replication
q1.description: How does MySQL master-slave replication work at scale?

metadata.tags: concept/async, concept/legacy, data/state, data/stream, database/mysql, distributed/quorum, interview-technical, performance/latency, scaling/replication, system/architecture, system/recovery, testing/code
metadata.mermaid:
graph TB Master["Master
Writes"] BinLog["Binary Log
Change Stream"] Slave1["Slave 1
Reads"] Slave2["Slave 2
Reads"] Slave3["Slave 3
Reads"] Master -->|Write| BinLog BinLog -->|Replicate| Slave1 BinLog -->|Replicate| Slave2 BinLog -->|Replicate| Slave3 Slave1 -->|< 1 sec lag| BinLog Slave2 -->|< 1 sec lag| BinLog Slave3 -->|< 1 sec lag| BinLog style Master fill:#bbdefb style BinLog fill:#90caf9 style Slave1 fill:#64b5f6 style Slave2 fill:#42a5f5 style Slave3 fill:#2196f3
metadata.answer:
## MySQL Architecture & Replication

### Storage Engines

**InnoDB** (default):
- ACID transactions
- Row-level locking
- Crash recovery
- Foreign keys
- Good for OLTP

**MyISAM** (legacy):
- Table-level locking
- No transactions
- Faster inserts (no ACID cost)
- Rarely use new code

### Replication Architecture
```
Master (writes) → Binlog (change stream)
  ├→ Slave 1 (reads)
  ├→ Slave 2 (reads)
  └→ Slave 3 (reads)

Slave copies binlog, applies changes
Typical lag: < 1 second
```

### Replication Process
```
1. Master writes binlog
2. Slave I/O reads binlog
3. Slave writes relay log
4. Slave SQL executes relay log
5. Slave state updates
```

### Binary Log Formats
- **STATEMENT**: Log SQL (fastest, may diverge)
- **ROW**: Log changes (safest, larger)
- **MIXED**: STATEMENT default, ROW for unsafe

### Replication Modes
- **Async**: Master doesn't wait (risk: data loss)
- **Semi-sync**: Master waits 1 slave ack (safer)
- **Group Replication**: Multi-master quorum (5.7.6+)

### Monitor Slave
```sql
SHOW SLAVE STATUS\G
-- Seconds_Behind_Master: latency
-- Last_Error: troubleshooting
SHOW MASTER STATUS;
```

### Performance Tuning
```sql
SET SLAVE_PARALLEL_WORKERS = 4;  -- Parallel replication (8.0)
SHOW PROCESSLIST;  -- Monitor replication
```

q2.label: MySQL Sharding Strategies & Challenges
q2.description: How do you shard MySQL databases for massive scale?

metadata.tags: concept/values, database/mysql, design/middleware, design/solution, interview-technical, quality/limits, scaling/sharding, system/consistency
metadata.mermaid:
graph TB Client["Client"] Router["Router"] Shard1["Shard 1
1-100M"] Shard2["Shard 2
100M-200M"] Shard3["Shard 3
200M-300M"] Hash["hash(user_id)
% shards"] Client -->|Query| Router Router --> Hash Hash -->|Shard 0| Shard1 Hash -->|Shard 1| Shard2 Hash -->|Shard 2| Shard3 style Client fill:#ffccbc style Router fill:#ffab91 style Hash fill:#ff8a65 style Shard1 fill:#ff7043 style Shard2 fill:#f4511e style Shard3 fill:#e64a19
metadata.answer:
## MySQL Sharding Strategies & Challenges

### Overview
```
Client → Router → Shard 1
            ├→ Shard 2
            └→ Shard 3
```

### Shard Key Selection

**Good**:
```sql
-- High cardinality, distributed
Shard by: user_id (millions of values)
```

**Bad**:
```sql
-- Low cardinality → uneven
Shard by: country (200 values)

-- Hotspot → 99% one shard
Shard by: status ('active', 'inactive')
```

### Strategy 1: Range-Based
```
Shard 1: user_id 1-100M
Shard 2: user_id 100M-200M
Shard 3: user_id 200M-300M
```
Pros: Simple, range scans
Cons: Hotspots possible

### Strategy 2: Hash-Based
```
Shard = hash(user_id) % num_shards
Even distribution guaranteed
```
Pros: No hotspots
Cons: Range queries slow (all shards)

```python
import hashlib
def get_shard(user_id, num_shards):
    hash_val = int(hashlib.md5(str(user_id).encode()).hexdigest(), 16)
    return hash_val % num_shards
```

### Strategy 3: Directory-Based
```sql
CREATE TABLE shard_directory (
  user_id INT,
  shard_id INT,
  PRIMARY KEY (user_id)
);

SELECT shard_id FROM shard_directory WHERE user_id = 12345;
```
Pros: Flexible, rebalance-friendly
Cons: Extra lookup, centralized

### Challenges

**Cross-Shard Transactions**:
- Impossible in standard SQL
- Solution: Application-level coordination or eventual consistency

**Joins Across Shards**:
- Not supported
- Solution: Denormalize, fetch separately

**Rebalancing**:
- Adding shard changes hash distribution
- Solution: Consistent hashing or directory lookup

**Hotspots**:
- Celebrity user concentrates traffic
- Solution: Secondary sharding or accept

### Middleware
- **MySQL Router**: Automatic InnoDB Cluster sharding
- **Vitess**: YouTube's sharding proxy (massive scale)
- **ProxySQL**: Query-aware routing

### Checklist
- [ ] High-cardinality shard key
- [ ] Plan rebalancing strategy
- [ ] Accept cross-shard query limits
- [ ] Monitor hotspots
- [ ] Document routing logic

q3.label: Query Optimization & Index Design
q3.description: How do you optimize slow MySQL queries?

metadata.tags: algorithm/tree, database/mysql, interview-technical, pattern/design, performance/indexing, performance/optimization, system/monitoring
metadata.mermaid:
graph TD Query["Query:
WHERE email=?"] Planner["Query Planner"] Index["Index
Scan"] Table["Table
Scan"] Choose["Choose Best
Path"] Execute["Execute"] Query --> Planner Planner -->|Cost
estimate| Index Planner -->|Cost
estimate| Table Index -->|Cost: 1 row| Choose Table -->|Cost: 1M rows| Choose Choose -->|Pick Index| Execute style Query fill:#f3e5f5 style Planner fill:#e1bee7 style Index fill:#ce93d8 style Table fill:#ba68c8 style Choose fill:#ab47bc style Execute fill:#8e24aa
metadata.answer:
## Query Optimization & Index Design

### Index Types

**Primary Key** (Clustered):
```sql
CREATE TABLE users (
  id INT PRIMARY KEY AUTO_INCREMENT,
  name VARCHAR(255)
);
-- InnoDB: All data in primary key B-tree
```

**Secondary Index**:
```sql
CREATE INDEX idx_email ON users(email);
-- Separate B-tree, points back to primary key
```

**Composite Index**:
```sql
CREATE INDEX idx_status_created ON orders(status, created_at);
-- WHERE status=? AND created_at>? uses index
-- WHERE created_at>? doesn't use (wrong order)
```

**Covering Index** (Has all needed columns):
```sql
CREATE INDEX idx_user_email_name ON users(user_id, email, name);
-- SELECT email, name FROM users WHERE user_id=? (no table lookup)
```

**Full-Text**:
```sql
CREATE FULLTEXT INDEX idx_content ON articles(content);
SELECT * FROM articles WHERE MATCH(content) AGAINST('MySQL');
```

### Query Execution Plan
```sql
EXPLAIN SELECT * FROM users WHERE email='john@example.com';

type: const (1 row) > ref (multiple) > range > index > ALL (full scan)
```

### ESR Rule: Equality → Sort → Range
```sql
-- For: WHERE status='pending' AND created_at>date ORDER BY amount DESC
CREATE INDEX idx_esr ON orders(status, amount, created_at);
--                             ├─ Equality
--                             ├─ Sort DESC
--                             └─ Range
```

### Anti-patterns
- ❌ NULL in WHERE (use IS NULL)
- ❌ Functions in WHERE (breaks index)
- ❌ OR (use BETWEEN)

### Monitoring
```sql
-- Unused indexes
SELECT * FROM INFORMATION_SCHEMA.STATISTICS
WHERE TABLE_SCHEMA='mydb'
GROUP BY SEQ_IN_INDEX=1
HAVING SUM(STAT_VALUE)=0;

-- Check size
SELECT INDEX_NAME, STAT_VALUE * @@innodb_page_size / 1024 / 1024 as size_mb
FROM INFORMATION_SCHEMA.INNODB_STATS
WHERE STAT_NAME='size';
```

### Best Practices
- [ ] Index high-cardinality first
- [ ] Keep indexes narrow
- [ ] Remove duplicate indexes
- [ ] Use EXPLAIN always
- [ ] Monitor performance
- [ ] Avoid over-indexing

q4.label: Connection Pooling & Resource Management
q4.description: How do you manage database connections efficiently?

metadata.tags: database/mysql, interview-technical, language/python, quality/limits, quality/management, quality/resource
metadata.mermaid:
graph TB App["Application
Threads"] Pool["Connection Pool
32 connections"] Available["Available
Connections"] InUse["In Use
Connections"] MySQL["MySQL
Server"] App -->|get_connection| Pool Pool -->|Available?| Available Pool -->|In use?| InUse Available -->|Reuse| App InUse -->|Wait| Available Available -->|Execute| MySQL style App fill:#fff3e0 style Pool fill:#ffe0b2 style Available fill:#ffd54f style InUse fill:#ffca28 style MySQL fill:#ffc107
metadata.answer:
## Connection Pooling & Resource Management

### Why Pooling?
```
Open conn: 100-200ms (expensive)
Query: < 1ms
Without pool: 99% time in connection overhead
```

### Pool Configuration
```python
pool = mysql.connector.pooling.MySQLConnectionPool(
    pool_size=32,        # (Cores × 2) + Spindle Count
    pool_reset_session=True,
    host="localhost"
)
```

### Guidelines
```
Pool Size = (Core Count × 2) + Effective Spindle Count
Example: 4-core → 8-16 connections
```

### Connection States
```
Available → In Use → Available
    ↓
Idle (timeout) → Closed

Timeout: 5-10 minutes typical
```

### Prepared Statements
```python
cursor = connection.cursor(prepared=True)
query = "SELECT * FROM users WHERE id = %s"
cursor.execute(query, (user_id,))
# Reuse: parse once, data changes
```

### Multi-threaded Pool
```python
import concurrent.futures

def query_db(user_id):
    conn = pool.get_connection()
    cursor = conn.cursor()
    cursor.execute("SELECT * FROM users WHERE id = %s", (user_id,))
    return cursor.fetchone()

with concurrent.futures.ThreadPoolExecutor(max_workers=10) as executor:
    futures = [executor.submit(query_db, i) for i in range(100)]
    results = [f.result() for f in futures]
```

### Prevent Leaks
```python
# BAD: Forgot close
def get_user(user_id):
    conn = mysql.connector.connect(...)
    return conn.execute(...)

# GOOD: Context manager
def get_user(user_id):
    with pool.get_connection() as conn:
        return conn.execute(...)  # Auto-closes
```

### MySQL Limits
```sql
SHOW VARIABLES LIKE 'max_connections';  -- Default: 100
SET GLOBAL max_connections = 1000;
SHOW STATUS LIKE 'Threads_connected';
SHOW STATUS LIKE 'Max_used_connections';
```

### Best Practices
- [ ] Pool size = (Cores × 2) + 4
- [ ] Monitor active connections
- [ ] Use prepared statements
- [ ] Timeout < 30s
- [ ] Close explicitly
- [ ] Use context managers
- [ ] Test load (JMeter)

### Metrics to Monitor
- Active / pool size
- Acquire time
- Execute time
- Idle time
- Connection leaks
- Failed attempts

q5.label: Backup, Recovery & Data Durability
q5.description: What's the strategy for MySQL backups and disaster recovery?

metadata.tags: data/replication, database/mysql, interview-technical, system/recovery
metadata.mermaid:
graph LR Full["Full Backup
Sunday"] Inc1["Incremental
Mon-Wed"] Inc2["Incremental
Thu-Sat"] BinLog["Binlog
Continuous"] Recovery["Point-in-Time
Recovery"] Full -->|Base| Inc1 Inc1 -->|Incremental| Inc2 Inc2 -->|+ Binlog| Recovery BinLog -->|Every sec| Recovery style Full fill:#c8e6c9 style Inc1 fill:#a5d6a7 style Inc2 fill:#81c784 style BinLog fill:#66bb6a style Recovery fill:#4caf50
metadata.answer:
## Backup, Recovery & Data Durability

### Backup Types

**Full Backup**:
```bash
mysqldump --all-databases > full_backup.sql
xtrabackup --backup --target-dir=/backup/full
```
Frequency: Weekly
Time: Depends on size

**Incremental**:
```bash
xtrabackup --backup --target-dir=/backup/incr1 --incremental-basedir=/backup/full
```
Captures: Only changes since last
Frequency: Daily
Size: 1-5% of full

**Binlog**:
```bash
cp /var/log/mysql/mysql-bin.* /backup/binlogs/
```
Enables: Point-in-time recovery
Frequency: Continuous

### Recovery Scenarios

**Single Table**:
```bash
xtrabackup --prepare --target-dir=/backup/full
xtrabackup --copy-back --tables=db.table --target-dir=/backup/full
mysqlbinlog mysql-bin.000010 | mysql
```
Time: 30 min - 2 hours

**Full Server**:
```bash
xtrabackup --copy-back --target-dir=/backup/full
mysqlbinlog mysql-bin.000010 mysql-bin.000011 | mysql
```
Time: 1-6 hours

**Point-in-Time**:
```bash
mysqlbinlog --start-datetime="2023-06-15 10:00:00" \
           --stop-datetime="2023-06-15 11:00:00" \
           mysql-bin.* | mysql
```

### Binary Log Retention
```sql
SET GLOBAL binlog_expire_logs_seconds = 864000;  -- 10 days
PURGE BINARY LOGS BEFORE '2023-06-01 00:00:00';
SHOW BINARY LOGS;
```

### Durability Settings
```sql
-- Maximum durability (slower)
SET GLOBAL innodb_flush_log_at_trx_commit = 1;

-- Fast but risky
SET GLOBAL innodb_flush_log_at_trx_commit = 2;  -- Buffer OS
SET GLOBAL innodb_flush_log_at_trx_commit = 0;  -- Buffer 1 sec

-- Double-write buffer (corruption prevention)
SET GLOBAL innodb_doublewrite = 1;  -- Default
```

### Backup Strategy
```
Week: Full (Sunday)
      ├─ Incremental (Mon-Sat)
      ├─ 3 locations
      └─ Test restore weekly

Binlog: Continuous, rotate hourly, compress daily, keep 30 days

RPO: < 5 minutes
RTO: < 1 hour
```

### Replication as Backup
```
Primary → Slave 1 (backup, never write)
       → Slave 2 (hot standby)
```

### Checklist
- [ ] Full backup weekly
- [ ] Incremental daily
- [ ] Test restore monthly
- [ ] Keep 30 days binlogs
- [ ] Offsite backup (S3)
- [ ] Encrypt backups
- [ ] Monitor job success
- [ ] Document procedures

### GTID Tracking
```sql
SET GLOBAL gtid_mode = 'ON';
SHOW MASTER STATUS;  -- executed_gtid_set
CHANGE MASTER TO MASTER_AUTO_POSITION = 1;
```

q6.label: MySQL vs PostgreSQL Comparison
q6.description: When do you choose MySQL vs PostgreSQL?

metadata.tags: cloud/aws, company/values, concept/async, data/partitioning, data/replication, data/sharding, database/mysql, database/postgresql, design/decision, interview-technical, performance/throughput, system/scalability
metadata.mermaid:

    E["Decision"]

    C --> B
    style A fill:#bbdefb
    style C fill:#bbdefb

metadata.answer:
## MySQL vs PostgreSQL Comparison

### Feature Matrix

| | MySQL | PostgreSQL |
|---|---|---|
| Open Source | Yes | Yes |
| ACID | InnoDB: Yes | Yes |
| JSON | Good | Excellent (JSONB) |
| Window Functions | 8.0+ | Yes |
| CTEs | 8.0+ | Yes |
| Partitioning | Good | Good |
| Replication | Async/Semi | Logical |
| Performance | Fast reads | Fast complex queries |

### Query Examples

**MySQL JSON**:
```sql
CREATE TABLE users (metadata JSON);
INSERT INTO users VALUES ('{"age": 30, "city": "NYC"}');
SELECT JSON_EXTRACT(metadata, '$.age') FROM users;
```

**PostgreSQL JSONB**:
```sql
CREATE TABLE users (metadata JSONB);
INSERT INTO users VALUES ('{"age": 30, "city": "NYC"}');
SELECT metadata->>'age' FROM users;
SELECT * FROM users WHERE metadata @> '{"city": "NYC"}';
```

### Use Cases

**Choose MySQL**:
- Large existing user base
- Simplicity important
- High write throughput
- Team familiar
- Cost-conscious

Companies: Uber, Twitter, Airbnb, Pinterest

**Choose PostgreSQL**:
- Complex queries needed
- Advanced data types
- Full-text search
- ACID critical
- Advanced features

Companies: Spotify, Instagram, Shopify, Cloudflare

### Scaling

**MySQL**:
- Master-slave replication
- Sharding (application)
- Vitess (YouTube layer)

**PostgreSQL**:
- Logical replication
- Citus (distributed)
- Table partitioning

### Migration

**MySQL → PostgreSQL**:
- NULL handling different
- Date functions differ
- Isolation levels differ
- Tools: pgloader, AWS DMS

**PostgreSQL → MySQL**:
- JSONB → JSON (less feature)
- Window functions → subqueries
- Type conversion needed

### Cost (1 TB)
- MySQL: $50-500/month RDS, $5-20K hardware
- PostgreSQL: Similar ($100-400/month, $5-20K hardware)

### Decision Matrix
```
Simple OLTP (write)        → MySQL
Complex queries            → PostgreSQL
High scalability           → MySQL (more tools)
Advanced features          → PostgreSQL
Team preference            → Choose that
Cost                       → Similar
```

q1.label: Pointers, References & Memory Management
q1.description: Compare C pointers with C++ references. Explain manual allocation/deallocation and common pitfalls.

metadata.tags: code-example, performance/indexing, quality/management
metadata.answer: At Google, a systems engineer explained: 'In C, we use pointers for everything—malloc/free pairs must match perfectly. We had a production bug where a double-free crashed the entire indexing service. In C++, we prefer references and smart pointers. std::unique_ptr enforces ownership, std::shared_ptr handles ref counting. Smart pointers prevent most memory leaks automatically. For example, in our distributed storage system, we replaced 2000+ manual delete calls with unique_ptr, eliminating 15+ memory-related bugs in 6 months.'

q2.label: Stack vs Heap Allocation & RAII
q2.description: Explain stack vs heap allocation, their performance characteristics, and RAII (Resource Acquisition Is Initialization) principle.

metadata.tags: algorithm/queue, algorithm/stack, code-example, compression/latency, data-structure/array, interview/database, quality/resource
metadata.answer: A Meta infrastructure engineer shared: 'Stack allocation is trivial—O(1), just move a pointer. At Ads scale, we millions of short-lived objects. Using stack_allocated std::array<T, N> instead of std::vector gave us 40% latency improvement in our batch processing pipeline. RAII means when the object goes out of scope, its destructor runs—files close, locks release, memory frees automatically. We wrapped database connections in a scoped_lock RAII wrapper. When an exception occurred, locks still released correctly, preventing deadlocks. This robustness is critical for financial trading systems.'

q3.label: STL Containers & Algorithmic Complexity
q3.description: Compare std::vector, std::list, std::map, std::unordered_map. When to use each and their performance trade-offs.

metadata.tags: algorithm/hash, code-example, design/api, optimization/complexity
metadata.answer: At Amazon, a service engineer explained: 'In our product catalog system, we initially used std::vector and binary_search for millions of SKUs. O(log N) lookups were fine, but insertion shifted all elements—O(N). When we switched to std::unordered_map with hash-based lookup, insertion became O(1) average. Our product insertion API went from 500ms to 5ms per batch. The trade-off: unordered_map uses more memory (hash table overhead) and has O(N) worst case if hash collisions happen. For our use case, the speed gain justified the extra memory.'

q4.label: Copy Semantics vs Move Semantics
q4.description: Explain copy constructors, move constructors, and copy-and-swap idiom. When does move semantics shine?

metadata.tags: algorithm/stack, code-example, performance/throughput
metadata.answer: A Stripe payment systems engineer told us: 'In our transaction processing pipeline, we pass large JSON payloads through 5+ processing stages. With copy semantics, each stage deep-copied the payload—megabytes of allocations. We implemented move semantics: std::move(payload) transfers ownership without copying. Then rvalue references in function signatures: void process(std::vector<uint8_t>&& data). One transaction went from 50 allocations to 2. At our transaction volume (millions/day), that's gigabytes of allocation pressure eliminated. Move semantics is essential for high-throughput systems.'

q5.label: Concurrency: Mutexes, Locks & Data Races
q5.description: Explain std::mutex, std::lock_guard, data races, and synchronization. How to debug race conditions?

metadata.tags: code-example, design/pattern, performance/throughput, quality/deployment, quality/testing
metadata.answer: A Google Search engineer described: 'Our query ranking pipeline had a subtle data race. Two threads accessed a shared cache without synchronization. Occasionally, rank scores were corrupted—users saw wrong results. We wrapped the cache with std::mutex. Reader threads use std::shared_lock (multiple readers), writers use std::unique_lock (exclusive). This read-write lock pattern improved throughput 3x because most queries are reads. Race conditions are invisible in testing—they appear randomly in production at scale. We now use ThreadSanitizer in CI to catch them before deployment.'

q6.label: Template Programming & Generic Code
q6.description: Explain templates, template specialization, and metaprogramming. What are the compilation costs and benefits?

metadata.tags: caching/latency, code-example, company/benefits, style/code
metadata.answer: A Bloomberg financial systems engineer explained: 'Our option pricing engine needed ultra-fast computation. We wrote a template function: template<typename T> T calculate_price(...). For each T (double, long double, float), the compiler generates optimized machine code. At compile-time, we know T's size and alignment—we can inline everything. A generic version via virtual functions would be 5x slower due to indirection. Trade-off: compilation time increased 30%, but runtime latency decreased 40%. For real-time trading, sub-millisecond performance is critical. We compile once, deploy many times, so the compilation cost is amortized.'

q7.label: Buffer Overflows, Security & Bounds Checking
q7.description: Explain buffer overflow vulnerabilities in C/C++. How to write secure code? What are static/dynamic protections?

metadata.tags: code-example, concept/legacy, pattern/array, quality/code
metadata.answer: A Microsoft security team member shared: 'We found a buffer overflow in legacy C code: char buffer[256]; strcpy(buffer, user_input). If user_input exceeded 256 bytes, it overwrote the return address, enabling code injection. We switched to strncpy and bounds checking. Modern C++ idiom: use std::string and std::array with size_t, never raw char[]. At compile time, enable -D_FORTIFY_SOURCE=2 (glibc bounds checks), use AddressSanitizer in dev. In production, enable DEP (Data Execution Prevention) and ASLR (address randomization). Buffer overflows are the #1 CVE category—discipline matters.'

q8.label: C vs C++: When to use C? Interoperability & Legacy Systems
q8.description: Compare C and C++ for systems programming. Why choose C? How to call C from C++ or vice versa?

metadata.tags: code-example, concept/legacy
metadata.answer: A Linux kernel engineer explained: 'The Linux kernel is pure C—few dependencies, maximum portability, minimal runtime overhead. System calls (read, write, mmap) are C. Device drivers are C. When we need a userspace system utility, C is perfect. But when building high-level services at Meta or Netflix, C++ shines: STL containers, exceptions, templates eliminate boilerplate. We call C libraries from C++ via extern "C" wrapper: extern "C" { int posix_function(); }. At Netflix, our CDN edge software is C++ calling OpenSSL (C library) for crypto. The extern "C" prevents name mangling—C linker sees the right symbol. Language choice is about ecosystem and constraints, not performance alone.'

metadata.answer: At Netflix, an engineer explained: 'Redis powers our session store. 50M active users, each with session data. We use Redis strings for serialized sessions with TTL. When a user logs in, we set their session: SET session:user123 {json} EX 86400. For rankings, we use sorted sets: ZADD leaderboard score user. When a movie trends, we increment counters with INCR. Redis is fast because it's in-memory, but data loss on restart means we use Redis only for ephemeral data. For persistent session fallback, we query DynamoDB.' At Uber, sorted sets handle driver rankings: ZRange to find nearest drivers by score. Streams store event logs—ride requests, driver updates—with consumer groups for exactly-once processing.

q2.label: Redis Replication, Clustering & Failover
q2.description: Explain Redis master-slave replication, sentinel for high availability, cluster mode for horizontal scaling, and consistency guarantees.

metadata.tags: cache/memcached, data/caching, data/replication, distributed/quorum, interview-technical, performance/throughput, quality/deployment, system/failover, system/recovery, system/scalability, timespace/complexity
metadata.answer:
## Replication Models

**Master-Slave Replication:**
- Master handles all writes; slaves replicate asynchronously
- Replication is non-blocking: master doesn't wait for slave ACK, enabling high write throughput
- Data loss risk on master crash: some unsynced writes may be lost
- Use case: read-heavy scenarios (Netflix sessions, Twitter caches)

**Synchronous vs Asynchronous:**
- Async: fast but risky. Master returns immediately, slave catches up later
- Sync (WAIT command): write waits for N slave ACKs before returning. Trade-off: slower writes but safer
- Example: `WAIT 1 0` waits for at least 1 slave to acknowledge before responding

## Sentinel for High Availability

**Monitoring & Failover:**
- Sentinel monitors master health; if master heartbeat stops for 30s, triggers quorum vote
- Quorum: majority of sentinels must agree before promoting slave
- Failover time: ~30s (acceptable for most non-critical data)
- Automatic slave promotion: new master elected, other slaves update replication target

**Multi-Sentinel Deployment:**
- Deploy 3+ sentinels for quorum safety
- Sentinels themselves are HA: if one sentinel dies, others continue monitoring
- Clients connect via sentinel (not directly to master)

## Cluster Mode for Horizontal Scaling

**Hash Slot Distribution:**
- 16,384 hash slots; partition key determines slot: `hash(key) % 16384`
- Data evenly distributed across cluster nodes
- Client knows slot-to-node mapping via gossip protocol

**Resharding & Rebalancing:**
- Add new node: cluster redistributes slots automatically
- No downtime; live slot migration supports in-flight commands
- Remove node: slots migrate to remaining nodes

**Cross-Slot Operations:**
- Single key operations (GET, SET) route to correct node
- Multi-key commands (MGET) require all keys in same slot (use hash tags: `{user:123}:profile` and `{user:123}:session` both route to same slot)

## Consistency Guarantees

**Strong Consistency (Cluster, Sentinel):**
- Master-only writes ensure no conflicts
- Slave reads may be slightly stale (replication lag ~few ms)
- For critical data: read from master or use slave with lag monitoring

**Eventual Consistency:**
- Slave eventually syncs with master
- Acceptable for read-heavy, non-critical data (leaderboards, caches)

**Durability Modes:**
- RDB (snapshot): faster recovery, larger file size, data loss since last snapshot
- AOF (append-only file): every command logged, slower writes, no data loss
- Hybrid (RDB + AOF): balance of speed and safety

## Real-World Scenarios

**Facebook's Approach (billions of users):**
- Sentinel with 50-node Redis cluster
- Master for critical writes (user sessions, payment state)
- Slaves for read-heavy cache (posts, comments)
- WAIT 1 for sensitive transactions

**Netflix's Pattern:**
- Cluster mode for geographic distribution
- Sharded across regions; each region has sentinel-protected master
- Asynchronous replication acceptable for session caching (eventual consistency fine)

**Uber's Failover:**
- Driver location cached with 5-second TTL
- No replication needed (transient data)
- Memcached suffices; Redis for leaderboards (rider ratings, driver rankings) with sentinel

## Trade-offs

| Factor | Master-Slave | Sentinel | Cluster |
|--------|--------------|----------|----------|
| **Complexity** | Low | Medium | High |
| **Scalability** | Vertical (bigger master) | Vertical | Horizontal |
| **Failover Time** | Manual | ~30s auto | ~30s auto |
| **Consistency** | Strong (master) | Strong | Eventual |
| **Cost** | Low | Medium | High |
| **Best For** | Small deployments | HA critical data | Large scale |

q3.label: Memcached: Simple Caching & LRU Eviction
q3.description: Understand Memcached's simplicity vs Redis, LRU eviction policy, consistent hashing for client-side distribution, and when to use Memcached.

metadata.tags: cache/memcached, cache/redis, data/caching, data/replication, interview-technical
metadata.answer: At Twitter, a cache engineer explained: 'Memcached is dead simple: get/set/delete. No persistence, no replication, no data structures—just byte strings. When memory fills, LRU eviction removes the least recently used item. We shard data across memcached servers using consistent hashing: hash(key) determines which server. If a server dies, we rehash and repopulate from DB. Memcached is faster than Redis for simple caching because no parsing overhead. But we can't do sorted sets or lists. For session caching (strings only), Memcached is perfect. For leaderboards or job queues, we need Redis.' At LinkedIn, 50% cache misses on startup due to eviction—they tolerate this because DB fallback is fast. Memcached scales linearly: add more servers, add more capacity.

q4.label: DynamoDB: Serverless Key-Value on AWS
q4.description: DynamoDB: partition keys, sort keys, provisioned vs on-demand billing, global secondary indexes, stream processing.

metadata.tags: concept/values, data/stream, database/dynamodb, interview-technical, interview/fundamentals, performance/indexing, scaling/replication, scaling/sharding, search/elasticsearch, system/architecture, system/failover, system/split
metadata.answer:
## DynamoDB Fundamentals

**Serverless Architecture:**
- No servers to manage; AWS handles provisioning, scaling, and failover
- Pay per request (on-demand) or reserve capacity (provisioned)
- Automatic replication across 3 AZs in a region for durability
- Supports strong consistency (read-your-write) and eventual consistency

**Primary Keys:**
- **Partition Key (Hash Key)**: Determines which partition stores the item. Example: UserId. Must be unique or paired with sort key
- **Sort Key (Range Key)**: Optional. Orders items within partition. Example: Timestamp. Partition key + sort key = composite primary key (globally unique)
- Query efficiently: partition key required; sort key optional. Without partition key, requires full table scan (expensive)

## Capacity Models

**Provisioned Capacity (WCU/RCU):**
- WCU (Write Capacity Unit): 1 WCU = 1 write per second (up to 1KB item)
- RCU (Read Capacity Unit): 1 RCU = 1 strongly consistent read per second (up to 4KB item)
- Billing: pay per hour for reserved capacity, regardless of usage
- Example: 100 WCU = can write 100 items/sec (1KB each) = 36M writes/day
- Scaling: change capacity instantly (autoscaling available)

**On-Demand Capacity:**
- Pay per request: $1.5 per million writes, $0.375 per million reads
- No capacity planning; scales automatically to traffic
- Good for unpredictable workloads or low volume
- Expensive at scale (100K writes/sec provisioned = $80/month, on-demand = $4320/month)

## Indexing & Queries

**Global Secondary Index (GSI):**
- Alternate primary key for querying; partition and sort key different from table
- Example: Table partitioned by UserId; GSI partitioned by Email (fast queries by email)
- GSI consumes separate capacity (separate WCU/RCU)
- Sparse index: only items with GSI key included
- Eventually consistent by default (can enable strong consistency, higher cost)

**Local Secondary Index (LSI):**
- Same partition key as table; different sort key
- 10GB limit per partition key value (beware of hot partitions)
- Uses table's capacity (shares WCU/RCU)
- Must define at table creation; cannot add later

## Data Consistency & Replication

**Strong Consistency:**
- Read returns latest written value
- Reads all 3 AZ replicas to confirm majority sync
- Higher latency, higher RCU cost (2x)
- Use for: financial transactions, user passwords, critical state

**Eventual Consistency (default):**
- Read may return stale data (< 1 second lag)
- Reads one AZ replica
- Lower latency, lower RCU cost (1x)
- Use for: user profiles, cache, non-critical data

## Streams & Processing

**DynamoDB Streams:**
- Ordered log of item changes (INSERT, UPDATE, DELETE)
- Retention: 24 hours
- Shards: automatically split based on throughput
- Triggers: Lambda functions, Kinesis, external systems
- Example: user updates profile → stream record → Lambda → update Elasticsearch

**Global Tables:**
- Replicate table across regions automatically
- Multi-master: write to any region, sync to all
- Latency: single-digit milliseconds for reads
- Consistency: eventually consistent across regions (default)

## Performance Optimization

**Hot Partitions:**
- If partition key is skewed (e.g., all writes to UserID=1), partition gets throttled
- Solution: add random suffix to partition key (UserID#shard0, UserID#shard1)
- Distribute writes across multiple shards, query all shards on read

**Item Size:**
- Max item size: 400KB
- Larger items consume more RCU/WCU
- Example: 1KB item with eventually consistent read = 1 RCU; 4KB = 1 RCU; 5KB = 2 RCU (rounded up to 4KB)

**Batch Operations:**
- BatchGetItem: get up to 16MB, 100 items (no filtering)
- BatchWriteItem: write up to 25 items (max 16MB)
- More efficient than individual requests
- Partial failures possible: retry failed items

**TTL (Time-To-Live):**
- Automatic item expiration after specified timestamp
- No additional cost; items deleted in background (~24 hours after TTL)
- Use for: sessions, temporary data, cache entries

## Trade-offs

| Aspect | Provisioned | On-Demand |
|--------|-------------|----------|
| **Cost (small load)** | $30+/month | <$5/month |
| **Cost (large load)** | $200/month | $10K+/month |
| **Predictability** | Known cost | Variable |
| **Scaling** | Manual or autoscaling | Instant automatic |
| **Best for** | Predictable, sustained load | Spiky, unpredictable |

## Real-World Architecture

**Startup (0-1M users):**
- On-demand capacity (no planning needed)
- Single region
- TTL for user sessions
- GSI for queries by email/phone

**Growth (1M-100M users):**
- Provisioned capacity with autoscaling
- Global tables for multi-region
- Streams → Lambda for real-time processing (leaderboards, notifications)
- GSI sharding for hot partitions

**Scale (100M+ users):**
- Multiple shards per logical entity (UserID#0, UserID#1)
- DynamoDB Streams → Kinesis → Spark for analytics
- Global tables with multi-region write strategy
- Cache layer (ElastiCache Redis) in front for hot reads

q5.label: Caching Strategies: Write-Through, Write-Back, TTL
q5.description: Explain cache-aside, write-through, write-back patterns, TTL/expiration, cache invalidation, and handling stampedes.

metadata.tags: algorithm/queue, compression/latency, concept/async, design/pattern, interview-technical, performance/caching
metadata.answer: At Google, a distributed systems engineer shared: 'We use write-back caching for speed. Write hits cache immediately (return in 1ms), then async job flushes to BigTable (async, 100ms). On cache miss, we experience slight latency but data consistency is eventual—acceptable for non-critical data. For user sessions (critical), we use write-through: write cache + Spanner (sync), higher latency but safe. TTL (time-to-live) is critical: user profile TTL=1 hour means stale data max 1 hour, acceptable. Cache invalidation is hard—we use pattern-based invalidation: user:123:* invalidates all keys starting with user:123. Cache stampede: 10K requests hit cache, all miss at same time (TTL expired), all hammer DB. Solution: use a probabilistic early expiration or distributed lock.'

q6.label: Redis vs Memcached vs DynamoDB: Trade-offs
q6.description: Compare Redis, Memcached, and DynamoDB on speed, features, persistence, scalability, cost, and when to choose each.

metadata.tags: cache/memcached, data/streaming, database/dynamodb, database/postgresql, infra/docker, interview-technical, interview/architecture, scaling/partitioning, scaling/sharding, security/encryption, system/failover, system/recovery, system/scalability, timespace/complexity
metadata.answer:
## Latency & Speed Comparison

**Redis:**
- In-memory, sub-millisecond latency (μs range)
- No persistence overhead if AOF/RDB disabled
- Fastest for reads and writes on a single node
- Replication adds ~few milliseconds lag between master-slave

**Memcached:**
- Slightly faster than Redis on simple GET/SET (no parsing overhead)
- No persistence, no replication complexity
- Distributed hashing handles scaling (stateless)

**DynamoDB:**
- Managed service, higher latency (~10-100ms)
- Network hop to AWS adds overhead
- Acceptable for most web services (API responses are typically >100ms anyway)
- Auto-scaling handles traffic spikes transparently

## Features & Data Structures

| Feature | Redis | Memcached | DynamoDB |
|---------|-------|-----------|----------|
| **Strings** | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ |
| **Lists** | ✓ | ✗ | ✗ |
| **Sets** | ✓ | ✗ | ✗ |
| **Sorted Sets** | ✓ | ✗ | ✗ |
| **Hashes** | ✓ | ✗ | ✗ |
| **Streams** | ✓ | ✗ | ✗ |
| **Secondary Index** | ✗ | ✗ | ✓ (GSI) |
| **Transactions** | ✓ | ✗ | ✓ |
| **TTL/Expiration** | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ |

**Redis Strengths:**
- Leaderboards: ZRANGE/ZREVRANGE for sorted sets
- Queues: LPUSH/RPOP for job queues
- Session store: HSET for user data, TTL for expiry
- Streams: ordered append-only log for event processing
- Pub/Sub: message broadcasting

**Memcached Strengths:**
- Simplicity: only GET/SET/DELETE
- Stateless: perfect for stateless caches (DB query cache)
- Linear scaling: add more nodes, add more capacity

**DynamoDB Strengths:**
- Global Secondary Index: query by email instead of user ID
- Streams: DynamoDB Streams trigger Lambda for serverless processing
- Fine-grained access control: IAM policies
- Multi-region replication: built-in global tables

## Persistence & Durability

**Redis:**
- RDB (snapshot): point-in-time backup, data loss since last snapshot
- AOF (append-only file): every command logged, higher durability, higher write overhead
- Hybrid mode (Redis 7+): both RDB and AOF for speed + safety
- Replication: backup to slaves in same region or across regions

**Memcached:**
- No persistence: data loss on restart
- Used only for caches (DB fallback acceptable)

**DynamoDB:**
- Fully persistent: data replicated across 3 AZs in region
- Point-in-time recovery: restore to any time in last 35 days
- Global tables: replicate across regions for disaster recovery

## Scalability Model

**Redis:**
- Vertical scaling: larger instance, more RAM, more CPU
- Horizontal scaling (cluster mode): manual sharding or Redis Cluster (16K hash slots)
- Master capacity bottleneck: single master handles all writes
- For 1M writes/sec: need very large master or multi-master sharding

**Memcached:**
- Horizontal scaling: consistent hashing distributes load across nodes
- Stateless: add/remove servers without downtime (with consistent hashing)
- No hot key problem: distribute uniformly
- For 1M ops/sec: add 100 memcached nodes (10K ops/sec each)

**DynamoDB:**
- Auto-scaling: provisioned mode (reserved throughput) or on-demand (pay per request)
- Partitioning: transparent sharding handles 100K+ partitions
- Handles 1M writes/sec natively without config
- No manual capacity planning for scale

## Cost Model

**Redis (Self-Managed):**
- Infrastructure cost: EC2 instances, network, storage for RDB/AOF backups
- Scaling cost: linear (add more instances)
- Ops cost: monitoring, failover, patching, security updates
- Total for 1M req/sec: ~$10K/month (infrastructure) + ops labor

**Redis (AWS ElastiCache):**
- Managed version: $500-2000/month for single instance
- Replication/cluster add cost
- Reduces ops labor to ~10 hours/month

**Memcached (ElastiCache):**
- Similar cost to Redis, slightly cheaper ($400-1500/month)
- Less complex to operate

**DynamoDB:**
- Provisioned: pay for reserved capacity (WCU/RCU)
- On-demand: pay per request (~$1.5 per million writes)
- For 1M writes/day: ~$0.04/day = $1.20/month (on-demand)
- For 1M writes/sec peak (but idle off-peak): on-demand is cheaper
- Provisioned mode: ~$360/month for 10 WCU (100 writes/sec)

## When to Choose Each

### Choose Redis When:
1. **Leaderboards, rankings**: sorted sets with scores (user ratings, game points)
2. **Job queues**: reliable queue with LPUSH/RPOP, retry logic
3. **Session store**: fast, rich data structures (user profile with TTL)
4. **Real-time analytics**: sorted sets, HyperLogLog for cardinality
5. **Pub/Sub messaging**: broadcast events to multiple subscribers
6. **Streams**: ordered event log (order history, user activity)

**Red flags for Redis:**
- Data larger than available RAM (cluster mode mitigates but adds complexity)
- Requires strong consistency across regions (use DynamoDB global tables)
- Operations team unavailable (managed DynamoDB safer)

### Choose Memcached When:
1. **Simple cache layer**: DB query cache, API response cache
2. **Stateless services**: no data structure complexity needed
3. **Cost-sensitive, scale-out ready**: consistent hashing, linear scaling
4. **Stateless containers**: Kubernetes pods with ephemeral Memcached

**Red flags for Memcached:**
- Need persistence (data loss unacceptable)
- Complex data structure queries (leaderboards, sorted sets)
- Cache coherency required (no invalidation mechanism)

### Choose DynamoDB When:
1. **Serverless architecture**: Lambda, API Gateway integration
2. **Global app**: replication across regions built-in
3. **Managed ops**: no servers to manage, AWS handles failover
4. **Compliance**: fine-grained IAM control, encryption, audit logs
5. **Unpredictable traffic**: on-demand mode scales automatically
6. **Primary data store**: durability, ACID transactions, point-in-time recovery

**Red flags for DynamoDB:**
- Complex queries (use RDS PostgreSQL instead)
- Real-time latency critical (Redis faster)
- Cost predictability (on-demand mode unpredictable)

## Real-World Architecture Patterns

**Typical Startup (0-1M users):**
- DynamoDB for persistent data (users, orders)
- ElastiCache Memcached for DB caching
- Session store: DynamoDB or ElastiCache Redis (lightweight)

**Growth Phase (1M-100M users):**
- DynamoDB for users, transactions, analytics
- ElastiCache Redis for sessions, leaderboards, queues
- Memcached retired (Redis better)
- Add CDN for static cache

**Scale Phase (100M+ users):**
- DynamoDB global tables for multi-region
- Redis Cluster (sharded) for per-region caching
- Kafka for event streaming
- ElastiCache for hot data, local region reads

## Summary Trade-off Matrix

| Metric | Redis | Memcached | DynamoDB |
|--------|-------|-----------|----------|
| **Latency** | <1ms | <1ms | 10-100ms |
| **Features** | Rich | Minimal | Moderate |
| **Persistence** | Yes (optional) | No | Yes |
| **Scalability** | Cluster (manual) | Linear (auto) | Linear (auto) |
| **Cost (small)** | $500/mo | $400/mo | <$10/mo |
| **Cost (large)** | $5K/mo | $4K/mo | $5K+/mo |
| **Ops effort** | High | Low | Very low |
| **Best use** | Complex cache | Simple cache | Persistent data |

q7.label: Distributed Caching & Cache Coherency
q7.description: Managing consistency across multiple cache nodes, invalidation propagation, and eventual consistency.

metadata.tags: algorithm/queue, design/solution, interview-technical, language/python, performance/caching, queue/kafka, system/consistency
metadata.answer: At Facebook, a caching expert explained: 'Distributed cache across 1000 nodes. User updates profile on Node A, invalidates cache. But Node B might still serve stale data from its cache. Solution: use a message broker (Kafka) to broadcast invalidation. All nodes subscribe to invalidation topic, delete key on message. Eventual consistency: within ~100ms, all nodes clear the key. Next read refetches fresh data from DB. Cost: one DB hit per invalidation. For user profile (reads >> writes), this is fine. Alternative: use versioning—store version number, key:version:data, fast invalidation by incrementing version without deleting. But this wastes memory (old versions linger).'

metadata.code:
# Python
# Distributed cache invalidation via message queue
def update_user(user_id, data):
    db.update_user(user_id, data)  # Write to DB
    # Publish invalidation message
    queue.publish('cache-invalidation', {'key': f'user:{user_id}'})

# Subscriber on each cache node
def on_invalidate_message(msg):
    key = msg['key']
    cache.delete(key)  # All nodes delete
    # Next read will refetch from DB

docker_essentials.label: Docker: Images, Containers & Compose
docker_essentials.description: Docker fundamentals: Dockerfile, image layers, docker-compose for multi-container applications, best practices, and image optimization.

metadata.tags: interview-technical

q1.label: Dockerfile Best Practices & Layer Caching
q1.description: Writing efficient Dockerfiles: multi-stage builds, layer caching, reducing image size, avoiding common pitfalls.

metadata.tags: data/caching, infra/docker, interview-technical, language/go, language/javascript, language/python, review/code
metadata.answer: At Docker, an engineer explained: 'Layer caching is critical for build speed. Each RUN, COPY, FROM creates a layer. If layer hasn't changed, Docker reuses cached layer—instant build. Example: COPY requirements.txt before COPY code. When you edit code, RUN pip install uses cached layer (5s). If you COPY code first, then requirements.txt changes, pip install reruns (5m). Multi-stage builds: builder stage compiles everything, final stage copies only binaries. Node example: builder installs dev dependencies (600MB), final stage is 50MB (just runtime). Alpine base image: 5MB vs Ubuntu 80MB. At Netflix, optimizing Dockerfile reduced image size 10x (1GB → 100MB), 10x faster pushes, 10x faster pulls.'
metadata.mermaid:
graph TD
    A["Dockerfile Layers"]
    B["FROM base image"]
    C["RUN commands"]
    D["COPY files"]
    E["Layer Caching"]
    A --> B
    B --> C
    C --> D
    C --> E
    style A fill:#ffebee
    style B fill:#ffcdd2
    style C fill:#ef9a9a
    style D fill:#e57373
    style E fill:#ef5350
metadata.code:
# Go
# Multi-stage Go build
FROM golang:1.21 AS builder
WORKDIR /app
COPY . .
RUN CGO_ENABLED=0 go build -o app .

FROM alpine:latest
RUN apk --no-cache add ca-certificates
COPY --from=builder /app/app .
CMD ["./app"]

# JavaScript
# Multi-stage Dockerfile for Node
FROM node:18 AS builder
WORKDIR /app
COPY package*.json ./
RUN npm ci
COPY . .
RUN npm run build

FROM node:18-alpine
WORKDIR /app
COPY --from=builder /app/dist ./dist
COPY --from=builder /app/node_modules ./node_modules
CMD ["node", "dist/index.js"]

# Python
# Dockerfile best practices
FROM python:3.11-slim

# Create virtual env to reduce dependencies
RUN python -m venv /opt/venv
ENV PATH="/opt/venv/bin:$PATH"

# Install requirements before COPY code (cache layer)
COPY requirements.txt .
RUN pip install --no-cache-dir -r requirements.txt

# COPY code last (changes often, invalidates cache)
COPY app/ /app
WORKDIR /app

CMD ["python", "app.py"]

q2.label: Docker Compose: Multi-Container Orchestration
q2.description: docker-compose for local development and production: services, networks, volumes, environment variables, health checks.

metadata.tags: behavior/development, database/postgresql, infra/docker, interview-technical, review/code, system/microservices
metadata.answer: At GitHub, an engineer shared: 'docker-compose local dev environment mirrors production. docker-compose up starts API, DB, Redis, all networked. depends_on ensures DB starts before API. healthcheck waits for DB to be ready (5-second retries). Volumes mount code so edits reload instantly without rebuild. Environment variables pass secrets. In production, we use Kubernetes instead of compose—compose is single-host, Kubernetes multi-host. But locally, compose speeds up development: spin up full stack in 30s, tear down in 5s. We version docker-compose.yml in git, new devs run "docker-compose up" and have entire stack running—no manual setup.'
metadata.mermaid:
graph TD
    A["docker-compose"]
    B["Services"]  
    C["Networks"]
    D["Volumes"]
    E["Environment"]
    A --> B
    A --> C
    A --> D
    A --> E
    style A fill:#e3f2fd
    style B fill:#bbdefb
    style C fill:#90caf9
    style D fill:#64b5f6
    style E fill:#42a5f5
metadata.code:
# Go
# docker-compose for Go microservices
version: '3.8'
services:
  api-1:
    build:
      context: .
      dockerfile: Dockerfile.api
    environment:
      PORT: 8001
  
  api-2:
    build:
      context: .
      dockerfile: Dockerfile.api
    environment:
      PORT: 8002
  
  proxy:
    image: nginx:alpine
    ports: ['80:80']
    volumes:
      - ./nginx.conf:/etc/nginx/nginx.conf:ro

# JavaScript
# docker-compose with Node services
version: '3.8'
services:
  app:
    build: .
    ports: ['3000:3000']
    environment:
      - REDIS_URL=redis://redis:6379
    depends_on:
      - redis
  
  redis:
    image: redis:7-alpine
    ports: ['6379:6379']

networks:
  default:
    name: app-network

# Python
# docker-compose.yml
version: '3.8'
services:
  api:
    build: .
    ports:
      - "8000:8000"
    environment:
      - DATABASE_URL=postgres://db:5432/myapp
    depends_on:
      db:
        condition: service_healthy
    volumes:
      - ./app:/app  # Live reload
  
  db:
    image: postgres:15
    environment:
      POSTGRES_DB: myapp
      POSTGRES_PASSWORD: secret
    volumes:
      - postgres_data:/var/lib/postgresql/data
    healthcheck:
      test: ["CMD", "pg_isready", "-U", "postgres"]
      interval: 5s
      timeout: 5s
      retries: 5

volumes:
  postgres_data:

q3.label: Docker Image Optimization & Registry
q3.description: Reducing image size, pushing to registries (Docker Hub, ECR), scanning for vulnerabilities, and image tagging strategies.

metadata.tags: algorithm/optimization, data/stream, design/api, infra/docker, interview-technical, language/go, language/javascript, language/python
metadata.answer: At Amazon, an ECR engineer explained: 'Docker images can be massive (1GB+). Multi-stage builds reduce to 100MB. We use ECR (Elastic Container Registry) for private image storage. Image tagging: latest (unstable), v1.0.0 (stable), sha256:abc123 (immutable). Registry stores layers: push only new layers, skip unchanged (1GB image, 10MB change = 10MB push). Image scanning: Trivy or ECR native scanning detects CVEs in base image. If Alpine 3.17 has a vulnerability, rebuild image with Alpine 3.18 (new base image = new layer). At scale (millions of images), storage is major cost—we delete images older than 90 days. For production rollback, we keep last 10 image versions.'

q4.label: Container Networking: Bridge, Host, Custom Networks
q4.description: Docker networking modes, custom bridge networks, service discovery with DNS, port mapping, and communication between containers.

metadata.tags: algorithm/stack, concept/legacy, infra/docker, infra/kubernetes, interview-technical, interview/communication, system/networking
metadata.answer: At Uber, an infrastructure engineer explained: 'Docker bridge network creates isolated namespace. Containers on same network can reach each other by hostname via embedded DNS. API container pings 'postgres' → Docker's embedded DNS resolver (127.0.0.11:53) answers with postgres container's IP. Host networking bypasses bridge—container uses host's network stack, sees all host ports. User-defined bridge networks support DNS names; default bridge does not (legacy). Network drivers: bridge (isolated), host (host stack), overlay (multi-host, used by Kubernetes), macvlan (assign MAC address). For local dev, docker-compose creates default bridge with DNS. For production, Kubernetes handles networking via CNI plugins.'
metadata.mermaid:
graph TD
    A["🎯 Container Networking: Bridge, Host, Cust"]
    B["Definition"]
    C["Use Cases"]
    D["Best Practices"]
    E["Considerations"]
    
    A --> B
    A --> C
    A --> D
    A --> E
    
    style A fill:#e3f2fd
    style B fill:#bbdefb
    style C fill:#90caf9
    style D fill:#90caf9
    style E fill:#90caf9
metadata.code:
# JavaScript
// docker-compose creates automatic service discovery
services:
  app:
    image: node:18
    environment:
      - DB_HOST=postgres  # Resolves to postgres container IP
  
  postgres:
    image: postgres:15

# app container DNS queries 'postgres' → Docker daemon
# Docker daemon resolves to postgres container IP (172.17.0.2)
// fetch('http://postgres:5432') works automatically

# Python
import docker

client = docker.from_env()

# Create custom bridge network
network = client.networks.create(
    'myapp-network',
    driver='bridge',
    ipam=docker.types.IPAMConfig(
        pool_configs=[docker.types.IPAMPool(subnet='172.20.0.0/16')]
    )
)

# Run containers on network
api = client.containers.run(
    'myapp:1.0',
    name='api',
    networks=['myapp-network']
)

db = client.containers.run(
    'postgres:15',
    name='db',
    networks=['myapp-network']
)

# API can reach DB via hostname 'db'
# curl http://db:5432 (DNS resolves to container IP)

q5.label: Docker Security: Running as Non-Root, Secrets Management
q5.description: Container security best practices: non-root user, read-only filesystem, secrets handling, AppArmor/seccomp, and privilege escalation prevention.

metadata.tags: design/solution, framework/flask, infra/docker, infra/kubernetes, interview-technical, review/code
metadata.answer: At Google, a security engineer shared: 'Running as root inside container is risky. If container is compromised, attacker has root on container (and potentially host). Solution: create non-root user in Dockerfile, switch to it before CMD. Read-only filesystem prevents code injection. Secrets (passwords, API keys) must not be in Dockerfile—they leak into image history. Instead, pass secrets at runtime: docker secrets (Swarm), Kubernetes secrets, or env vars from external vault. Drop Linux capabilities: CAP_NET_ADMIN unnecessary for most apps—drop with --cap-drop=ALL. Use AppArmor or seccomp profiles to restrict syscalls. Image scanning (Trivy, Snyk) detects vulnerable base images. At scale, never run container as root—default deny model.'

q6.label: Docker Performance Tuning & Resource Limits
q6.description: CPU/memory limits, resource requests, cgroup management, and optimizing container performance at scale.

metadata.tags: infra/docker, interview-technical, language/javascript, language/python, quality/limits, quality/management, quality/resource
metadata.answer: At Lyft, a performance engineer explained: 'Containers run in cgroups—kernel-enforced resource limits. cpus=0.5 means container gets max 50% of one CPU core. If it tries to use more, kernel throttles (pauses execution until quota resets). memory=512M is hard limit—if container exceeds, kernel kills it (OOMKilled). Requests (reservations) are soft limits: scheduler guarantees this resource available. Limits are hard—enforced by kernel. On 16-core machine with cpus=0.5, container sees 16 CPUs but can only use equivalent of 0.5 cores. Requests prevent oversubscription: 100 containers, each reserve 0.25 CPU = 25 CPUs needed minimum. Without requests, scheduler packs 100 containers on 4 cores, causing thrashing. Monitor with docker stats: %CPU, %MEM, network I/O. At scale (10K containers), resource limits prevent one noisy neighbor from consuming all resources.'

q1.label: Kubernetes Deployments: Rolling Updates & Rollbacks
q1.description: Deployment strategies: rolling updates, blue-green, canary. MaxSurge, MaxUnavailable, readiness/liveness probes.

metadata.tags: algorithm/stack, infra/kubernetes, interview-technical, language/python, quality/deployment, quality/resource
metadata.answer: At Google, a Kubernetes engineer explained: 'Rolling updates minimize downtime. maxSurge=1 allows brief overprovisioning—4 pods during update (3 required + 1 temporary). maxUnavailable=0 ensures no pods are down. Readiness probe checks if pod is ready to serve traffic—don't send requests until /health returns 200. Liveness probe kills unhealthy pods—if /health fails 3x, kubelet terminates pod (deployment replaces it). At scale (1000 pods), rolling updates take time: ~5s per pod = 5000s = ~1.5 hours for gradual rollout. For faster updates, increase maxSurge, but risk temporary resource spike. Blue-green deployment: deploy green stack in parallel, switch traffic instantly (no rolling). Canary: route 5% traffic to new version, monitor, gradually increase—catches regressions early.'
metadata.mermaid:
graph TD
    A["Rolling Update"]
    B["Old Pod"]
    C["New Pod"]
    D["maxSurge=1"]
    E["maxUnavailable=0"]
    F["All New"]
    A --> D
    A --> E
    D --> B
    D --> C
    E --> C
    C --> F
    style A fill:#ffebee
    style B fill:#ffcdd2
    style C fill:#ef9a9a
    style D fill:#e57373
    style E fill:#ef5350
    style F fill:#d32f2f
metadata.code:
# Python
# Kubernetes Deployment with rolling update
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: app
spec:
  replicas: 3
  strategy:
    type: RollingUpdate
    rollingUpdate:
      maxSurge: 1        # 1 extra pod during update
      maxUnavailable: 0  # Keep all pods available
  template:
    spec:
      containers:
      - name: app
        image: myapp:v2
        readinessProbe:
          httpGet:
            path: /health
            port: 8000
          initialDelaySeconds: 5
          periodSeconds: 5
        livenessProbe:
          httpGet:
            path: /health
            port: 8000
          failureThreshold: 3
          periodSeconds: 10

# YAML
# kubectl apply -f deployment.yaml triggers rolling update
# 1. maxSurge=1: create 4th pod (original 3 + 1)
# 2. Kill oldest pod: now 3 pods (new version)
# 3. Create 4th pod: now 4 pods
# 4. Kill 2nd oldest: now 3 pods (new version)
# 5. Repeat until all 3 are new version
# Readiness probe: pod ready when /health returns 200
# Liveness probe: kill pod if /health fails 3x

# Rollback if update fails
# kubectl rollout undo deployment/app

q2.label: Kubernetes Services: ClusterIP, NodePort, LoadBalancer
q2.description: Service types and networking: internal (ClusterIP), external (NodePort, LoadBalancer), and service discovery via DNS.

metadata.tags: cloud/aws, concept/legacy, design/api, infra/kubernetes, interview-technical, interview/database, system/networking
metadata.answer: At Amazon EKS, a solutions architect explained: 'ClusterIP assigns internal IP (10.0.x.x), DNS name api.default.svc.cluster.local. Only accessible from within cluster. Useful for backend services (API talks to database). NodePort allocates port on every node (default random 30000-32767). External client reaches NodePort IP:port → routed to service → pod. NodePort is legacy; Ingress is modern. LoadBalancer provisions cloud load balancer (ELB on AWS), assigns external IP. External client reaches LB IP:80 → LB health checks pods → routed to running pods. At Uber, we use mostly ClusterIP internally, LoadBalancer for user-facing APIs. Service DNS: client.default.svc.cluster.local resolves to service IP, kube-proxy round-robins to healthy pods. Endpoint updates automatically when pods are added/removed.'
metadata.mermaid:
graph TD
    A["Kubernetes Services"]
    B["ClusterIP Internal"]
    C["NodePort External"]
    D["LoadBalancer Cloud"]
    A --> B
    A --> C
    A --> D
    style A fill:#e3f2fd
    style B fill:#bbdefb
    style C fill:#90caf9
    style D fill:#64b5f6
metadata.code:
# YAML
# ClusterIP: internal-only service
apiVersion: v1
kind: Service
metadata:
  name: api
spec:
  type: ClusterIP
  ports:
  - port: 80
    targetPort: 8000
  selector:
    app: api

# NodePort: expose on host port (random 30000-32767)
apiVersion: v1
kind: Service
metadata:
  name: api-external
spec:
  type: NodePort
  ports:
  - port: 80
    targetPort: 8000
    nodePort: 30080
  selector:
    app: api

# LoadBalancer: cloud provider allocates external IP
apiVersion: v1
kind: Service
metadata:
  name: api-lb
spec:
  type: LoadBalancer
  ports:
  - port: 80
    targetPort: 8000
  selector:
    app: api

q3.label: Kubernetes Ingress & API Gateway
q3.description: Ingress resources for HTTP/HTTPS routing, URL paths, virtual hosts, and load balancing.

metadata.tags: cloud/aws, design/api, infra/kubernetes, interview-technical, security/authentication, system/networking
metadata.answer: At Stripe, an infrastructure engineer explained: 'Ingress is layer 7 load balancer—routes by hostname and path. api.example.com/users → user-service, /products → product-service. nginx-ingress or AWS ALB controller watches Ingress resources, updates load balancer config. TLS termination at Ingress: cert-manager auto-renews certificates from Let's Encrypt. At scale, Ingress is single entry point for all HTTP traffic—reduces surface area. Can implement rate limiting, WAF, authentication at Ingress layer.'
metadata.mermaid:
graph TD
    A["🎯 Kubernetes Ingress & API Gateway"]
    B["Definition"]
    C["Use Cases"]
    D["Best Practices"]
    E["Considerations"]
    
    A --> B
    A --> C
    A --> D
    A --> E
    
    style A fill:#e3f2fd
    style B fill:#bbdefb
    style C fill:#90caf9
    style D fill:#90caf9
    style E fill:#90caf9
metadata.code:
# YAML
apiVersion: networking.k8s.io/v1
kind: Ingress
metadata:
  name: api-ingress
  annotations:
    cert-manager.io/cluster-issuer: letsencrypt
spec:
  ingressClassName: nginx
  tls:
  - hosts:
    - api.example.com
    secretName: api-tls
  rules:
  - host: api.example.com
    http:
      paths:
      - path: /v1/users
        pathType: Prefix
        backend:
          service:
            name: user-service
            port:
              number: 8000
      - path: /v1/products
        pathType: Prefix
        backend:
          service:
            name: product-service
            port:
              number: 8000

q4.label: Kubernetes Storage: PersistentVolumes & Claims
q4.description: StorageClass, PersistentVolume, PersistentVolumeClaim for stateful applications, and data persistence across pod restarts.

metadata.tags: cloud/aws, cloud/gcp, data/state, infra/kubernetes, interview-technical, interview/database
metadata.answer: At Digital Ocean, a database engineer explained: 'PersistentVolume abstracts underlying storage (local disk, EBS, NFS). PVC is a request—"I need 50Gi of fast storage." StorageClass defines provisioning: AWS EBS, GCP Persistent Disk, etc. StatefulSet uses volumeClaimTemplates: each pod gets dedicated PVC. Pod postgres-0 gets data-0, postgres-1 gets data-1. If pod restarts, same PVC reattaches—data persists. Databases use StatefulSet for this reason: pod identity stable (postgres-0.postgres.svc), storage stable (same PVC). Deployments are stateless: pod can be any replica, no persistent state. At scale, PVC creation is bottleneck—provision many PVCs in advance to avoid delays.'
metadata.mermaid:
graph TD
    A["🎯 Kubernetes Storage: PersistentVolumes & "]
    B["Definition"]
    C["Use Cases"]
    D["Best Practices"]
    E["Considerations"]
    
    A --> B
    A --> C
    A --> D
    A --> E
    
    style A fill:#e3f2fd
    style B fill:#bbdefb
    style C fill:#90caf9
    style D fill:#90caf9
    style E fill:#90caf9
metadata.code:
# YAML
# StorageClass defines storage backend
apiVersion: storage.k8s.io/v1
kind: StorageClass
metadata:
  name: fast-ssd
provisioner: kubernetes.io/aws-ebs
parameters:
  type: gp3
  size: 100Gi

# PersistentVolumeClaim requests storage
apiVersion: v1
kind: PersistentVolumeClaim
metadata:
  name: postgres-pvc
spec:
  storageClassName: fast-ssd
  accessModes:
    - ReadWriteOnce
  resources:
    requests:
      storage: 50Gi

# StatefulSet uses PVC for durable state
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: StatefulSet
metadata:
  name: postgres
spec:
  volumeClaimTemplates:
  - metadata:
      name: data
    spec:
      storageClassName: fast-ssd
      accessModes: [ReadWriteOnce]
      resources:
        requests:
          storage: 50Gi

q5.label: Kubernetes Operators & Helm Charts
q5.description: Custom Resource Definitions (CRDs), Operators for stateful services, and Helm for package management.

metadata.tags: database/postgresql, interview-technical, quality/deployment, quality/limits, quality/resource, system/failover, system/recovery, testing/code
metadata.answer:
## Kubernetes Operators

Operators extend Kubernetes with domain-specific knowledge by implementing the Operator pattern. They use Custom Resource Definitions (CRDs) to introduce new resource types and controllers to manage those resources.

### How Operators Work

- **CRD (Custom Resource Definition)**: Defines a new Kubernetes resource (e.g., `Database`, `Cache`, `Message Queue`)
- **Controller**: Watches CRD instances and implements reconciliation logic (desired state → actual state)
- **Example**: PostgreSQL operator allows `kubectl create database mydb --version=14` → operator creates StatefulSet, PVCs, ConfigMaps, handles backups and failover

### Operator Benefits

- Automates day-2 operations (upgrades, backups, scaling, failover)
- Encodes expertise: backup strategies, recovery procedures, tuning parameters
- Reduces manual intervention for stateful services
- Example: Prometheus operator converts Prometheus CRD → Deployment + Service + ConfigMap + RBAC

## Helm Charts

Helm is a package manager for Kubernetes that templatizes deployments and enables reusability.

### Chart Structure

```
mychart/
  Chart.yaml          # Metadata
  values.yaml         # Default values
  templates/
    deployment.yaml   # Uses {{ .Values.replicaCount }} interpolation
    service.yaml
    ingress.yaml
    configmap.yaml
```

### Helm Workflow

1. **Parameterize**: Chart templates use `.Values.*` placeholders
2. **Install**: `helm install myrelease ./mychart` → renders templates with values.yaml defaults
3. **Override**: `helm install myrelease ./mychart -f custom-values.yaml` → parameterize per environment
4. **Upgrade**: `helm upgrade myrelease ./mychart --version 2.0` → idempotent updates
5. **Rollback**: `helm rollback myrelease 1` → revert to previous release

### Helm Benefits

- Eliminates YAML duplication: one chart, many deployments (dev/staging/prod)
- Version management: track chart releases, rollback on failure
- Dependency management: `Chart.yaml` dependencies, `helm dependency update`
- Package sharing: Helm Hub, artifact repositories

## Trade-offs

### Operators vs Manual

| Operator | Manual Deployment |
|----------|-------------------|
| Complex logic (backups, failover) automated | Simpler initial setup |
| Encodes expertise, reduces human error | More operational burden |
| Steeper learning curve | Easier to understand |
| Requires CRD/controller maintenance | No custom code |

### Helm vs Kustomize vs Raw YAML

- **Helm**: Full templating, package management, large ecosystem (1000+ public charts)
- **Kustomize**: Lightweight, YAML overlay, no templating language, good for GitOps
- **Raw YAML**: Maximum control, no abstraction, tedious duplication

### When to use Operators

- **YES**: Complex stateful services (databases, caches, message queues)
- **YES**: Day-2 operations critical (backups, scaling, upgrades)
- **NO**: Simple stateless apps (use Helm)
- **NO**: One-off deployments (use kubectl apply + YAML)

### When to use Helm

- **YES**: Deploying multi-service applications (parameterized across environments)
- **YES**: Distributing applications (public charts, internal packages)
- **NO**: Micro-deployments (use kubectl apply)
- **NO**: Heavy customization needed (Kustomize may be better)
